items.foco: human immunodeficiency virus replicative cycle: key steps
items.title: Cycle steps
items.contentHtml:
human immunodeficiency virus replicative cycle: key steps

  • Binding: gp120 binds to CD4 and coreceptor (CCR5/CXCR4); fusion mediated by gp41.
  • Entry and decapsidation: release of the preintegration complex into cytoplasm.
  • Reverse transcriptase: converts viral RNA into double-stranded DNA (target step of NRTIs and NNRTIs).
  • Integration: integrase inserts proviral DNA into host genome (target of inhibitors). integrase).
  • Transcription and translation: viral mRNA produces Gag-Pol polyproteins.
  • Maturation: protease cleaves polyproteins into functional proteins (target of protease inhibitors).
  • Budding: immature particles released; They mature after protease action.

items.title: NRTIs: mechanism and drugs
items.contentHtml:
Reverse transcriptase: nucleoside inhibitors

NRTIs (nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors) act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into the elongating viral DNA, causing premature chain termination. They require intracellular triphosphorylation for their activation. Key drugs: tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) and tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) (less renal/bone toxicity), emtricitabine (FTC), lamivudine (3TC), abacavir (ABC) (associated with hypersensitivity reaction if HLA-B*5701 positive). Zidovudine (AZT) is already little used due to toxicity. Resistance due to M184V mutation confers high resistance to 3TC/FTC and low resistance to ABC.

items.title: Integrase inhibitors
items.contentHtml:
Integrase: function and inhibitors

The integrase catalyzes two steps: 3′-processing (cutting the 3′ end of the viral DNA) and strand transfer (insertion of the proviral DNA into the human genome). Integrase strand transfer inhibitors (INSTIs) block the second step. Drugs: dolutegravir (DTG), bictegravir (BIC), raltegravir (RAL), cabotegravir (CAB) (long-acting injection). DTG and BIC have a high genetic barrier. Adverse effect: increase in serum creatinine due to inhibition of the tubular transporter MATE-1 (not actual kidney damage). In pregnancy: DTG is associated with an increased risk of neural tube defects if used at conception.

items.title: Protease inhibitors
items.contentHtml:
Protease: viral maturation and drugs

The human immunodeficiency virus protease cleaves the Gag and Gag-Pol polyproteins into functional proteins (p24, p17, protease, integrase, reverse transcriptase). Protease inhibitors (PI): atazanavir (ATV), darunavir (DRV), lopinavir (LPV) (formulated with ritonavir as a booster). All PIs are metabolized by CYP3A4 and require augmentation with ritonavir (RTV) or cobicistat (COBI). Adverse effects: dyslipidemia, lipodystrophy, hyperbilirubinemia (ATV), PR/QT prolongation. The I50L mutation confers resistance to ATV. DRV/r is the PI of choice in rescue ART due to high genetic barrier.

items.title: Coreceptors and input
items.contentHtml:
Fusion and entry: CCR5/CXCR4 coreceptors

human immunodeficiency virus entry requires binding of gp120 to CD4 and subsequent interaction with a coreceptor: CCR5 (R5 tropism, predominant in transmission) or CXCR4 (X4 tropism, associated with progression). Maraviroc is a CCR5 antagonist; requires tropism test (Trofile) before use. The change from R5 to X4 (or mixed) tropism is associated with CD4 decline and worse prognosis. Enfuvirtide is a fusion inhibitor (gp41) for subcutaneous use, currently little used by other options. The V3 loop mutation determines tropism.

items.title: initial TAR of choice
items.contentHtml:
Recommended initial antiretroviral therapy

According to DHHS 2024 and GESIDA guidelines, the initial regimen of choice is a combination of two NRTIs plus an INSTI. Preferred options: BIC/TAF/FTC (Biktarvy®) and DTG/ABC/3TC (Triumeq®) if HLA-B*5701 negative. Alternative: DTG + TAF/FTC. Renal function, lipid profile and HBV coinfection should be evaluated. It is not recommended to start with IP/r or NNRTI as the first line due to greater toxicity or lower genetic barrier. In patients with viral load >500,000 copies/mL, avoid ABC due to greater risk of failure.

items.title: ART in pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Regimen of choice in pregnancy

The goal is undetectable viral load (<50 copies/mL) at delivery to prevent vertical transmission. Regimen of choice: two NRTIs (TAF/FTC or TDF/FTC; avoid TDF if kidney disease) plus one INSTI. DTG is associated with neural tube defects if exposed at conception (risk 0.19% vs. 0.11% baseline); If the patient is already on DTG and has an unplanned pregnancy, continue it. Alternative: darunavir/r + two NRTIs. Contraindicated: efavirenz (teratogen, category D) and dolutegravir at conception if there are options. In labor, AZT intravenous if viral load >1000 copies/mL.

items.title: PEP: duration and regimen
items.contentHtml:
Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) duration

PEP should be started within 72 hours (ideally in the first 2 hours) post-risk exposure (sexual, occupational, percutaneous). Duration: 28 consecutive days. Recommended regimen: tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) 300 mg + emtricitabine (FTC) 200 mg (Truvada®) once a day, plus raltegravir (RAL) 400 mg every 12 hours or dolutegravir (DTG) 50 mg/day. An human immunodeficiency virus test should be performed at the beginning, at 4-6 weeks and at 3 months. Don't forget to evaluate the risk of HBV and syphilis. If the source has known resistance, adjust the regime.

items.title: PrEP: indications
items.contentHtml:
Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) indications

PrEP is indicated in people at high risk of acquiring human immunodeficiency virus: men who have sex with men (MSM) without consistent condom use, serodiscordant couples, people with bacterial STI in the last 6 months, injection drug users. Oral regimen: TDF/FTC (Truvada®) or TAF/FTC (Descovy®) once daily. Injectable alternative: cabotegravir (Apretude®) every 2 months, with superior efficacy in MSM. Normal kidney function, negative human immunodeficiency virus test before starting and every 3 months is required. In women, only TDF/FTC has evidence; TAF/FTC not approved for receptive vaginal sex.

items.title: Primary resistance and genotype
items.contentHtml:
Primary resistance and genotyping

Primary (transmitted) resistance is detected by genotyping (RT, protease and integrase sequencing) before starting ART. Prevalence in Spain ~10-15%. Relevant mutations: M184V (high resistance to 3TC/FTC), K103N (resistance to efavirenz/nevirapine), L90M (IP). Genotyping is performed with viral load >1000 copies/mL. If there is virological failure, repeat genotype in therapy. INSTI resistance (for example R263K, G140S) is less common but emerging. Do not forget that resistance to tenofovir (mutation K65R) reduces sensitivity to TDF and ABC.

items.title: Acute retroviral syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Acute retroviral syndrome: manifestations

It occurs 2-4 weeks post-infection, coinciding with high viremia (>10⁶ copies/mL). Manifestations: fever (96%), lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis, maculopapular rash (trunk, symmetrical), myalgias/arthralgias, headache, mucocutaneous ulcers (genital, oral). Gastrointestinal symptoms: nausea, diarrhea. It can simulate infectious mononucleosis (but without exudative pharyngitis or marked splenomegaly). Diagnosis: human immunodeficiency virus-RNA detectable, p24 Ag positive and serology (ELISA/Western blot) initially negative or indeterminate. The CD4 count is usually temporarily low. Early recognition allows initiation of ART and reduces the reservoir.

items.foco: ART initiation criteria: CD4 and viral load
items.title: ART initiation: criteria
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for starting ART: CD4 and viral load

It is currently recommended to initiate ART in all patients with human immunodeficiency virus regardless of CD4 count (DHHS, WHO guidelines). The benefit is greater in CD4 <350 cells/μL (reduces morbidity and mortality). In asymptomatic patients with CD4 >500 cells/μL, early initiation reduces transmission (treatment as prevention strategy). Baseline viral load guides the choice of regimen: avoid abacavir if VL > 500,000 copies/mL. ART should be started as soon as possible, ideally within 7 days of diagnosis, except if there is active opportunistic infection (for example cryptococcal meningitis) where it is deferred 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).
